costModel.llmPer1kPosts: $5-15 depending on model tier (700 in / 450 out tokens/post)
costModel.llmLatencyPerPost: 1-4 s per call vs <1 ms baseline
costModel.operationalNote: At 1M posts/day the LLM cannot be the first-pass filter on cost alone. The defensible architecture is baseline-first triage with LLM evidence extraction only on the review-queue slice (~5-20% of volume), which is exactly how this lab wires it.

stanceShowdown.text: "A trading platform is offering 8% monthly returns on stablecoin deposits. They claim to be registered in Singapore, but I haven't verified the license."
stanceShowdown.llmAnalysis: Every risk phrase here is quoted from the platform being doubted ('they claim to be registered', 'I haven't verified'). Punishing the doubter teaches users to stop reporting suspicious platforms. The correct object of review is the platform, not the post.

stanceShowdown.text: "A platform claims to offer 'insured' crypto deposits with 10% monthly returns. The insurance provider is mentioned but I can't find their official website."
stanceShowdown.llmAnalysis: The speaker is evaluating a platform's claims, not making them: 'claims to offer', 'I can't find their official website'. The scam being described may well deserve investigation - the person describing it does not. Route the DESCRIBED platform to review; take no action against the poster.

stanceShowdown.text: "A crypto investment fund is accepting new investors with a $10K minimum. They claim 80% annual returns and have a professional website, but no regulatory filings visible."
stanceShowdown.llmAnalysis: An 80% annual return claim is being relayed with explicit doubt ('no regulatory filings visible'). The stance markers - hedging, absence of any ask from the speaker - separate reportage from promotion. A keyword system cannot see this; stance reading can.

perCase.reviewerSummary: Classic doubling scam: impersonated celebrity authority, a direct transfer request, an impossible instant return, and a countdown. All four signals are quoted verbatim; no benign context present.

Drainer-pattern lure: connect-wallet instruction tied to a free-ETH claim on an unverified domain with an expiry window. Domain behavior itself is unobservable, but the ask pattern alone meets the policy's strong-evidence bar.

Advance-fee recovery scam: universal recovery claim plus an upfront BTC fee with same-day promise. This typology re-victimizes people who already lost funds, which raises its severity under the enforcement matrix.
HYIP template: a guarantee, a risk-free claim, an AI-authority gloss, a deposit ask, and a midnight deadline. Every element of the guaranteed-return typology is present in one post.

Support-impersonation phishing: claims exchange authority, demands a deposit disguised as verification, and coerces with an account restriction. Real exchange support never requests deposits — the ask itself is dispositive.
Prize/fee scam: an unearned 2 BTC windfall gated behind a small upfront fee — the advance-fee template in giveaway costume.
Brand-impersonation grant scam: corporate authority claim, a BTC payment recast as identity validation, and an immediate bonus. Legitimate grants do not charge validation fees in crypto.
Mentor-funnel scam: daily-profit claim plus a DM funnel ending in deposit instructions. The recruitment surface of the fake-platform typology; the destination platform is deliberately kept off-post.
Ordinary earnings commentary. Mentions an exchange by name with no ask, no promise, and no funnel — the reason brand mentions alone must never be actionable.

Consumer warning that quotes scam vocabulary in order to warn against it. Labeling this would punish exactly the speech the platform most needs. Stance detection, not keyword detection, is what separates this from promotion.
Research description of scam mechanics. Names every scam feature while asking for nothing — protected research context.
Help-seeking about evaluating recovery services safely — the question form of the recovery topic, from a potential victim. Enforcement here would silence the people the policy protects.
Satire of doubling scams using explicitly imaginary assets and a self-negating authority claim. No real funds are solicited.
Developer debugging note. 'Wallet connection' appears in a technical stance with a testnet scope and no user-facing ask.
Price commentary with an explicit disclaimer and no ask. Ordinary financial speech.

Third-party description of an upcoming airdrop with the key evidence (the link) explicitly unverified. Could be a legitimate project or a drainer funnel; the text cannot decide it. Route to review — labeling would be premature, ignoring would be negligent.

A question about a contingency-fee recovery service. Contingency framing is a documented scam variant, but the speaker is asking, not offering. Review can check whether the named service recurs in the campaign graph.

Skeptical description of a paid-signals group. The described product matches the pump-group typology, but the speaker is evaluating, not promoting. Reviewer should assess the group, not the poster — the distinction the error analysis shows the baseline misses.

OCR fragment with a wallet-connect requirement and QR indirection but no observable source or destination. The ask pattern is drainer-like; the evidence quality is too degraded for a public label. Exactly what the review tier exists for.

interpretation: Where the two disagree, the interesting cases are stance cases: the baseline keys on WHAT vocabulary appears, the LLM on WHO is speaking and WHY. The error-analysis report shows the baseline's largest real false-positive class is skeptical third-party reportage — exactly the stance distinction an LLM reads natively. That, not aggregate accuracy on this small suite, is the argument for LLM assistance on the review slice. The one scored disagreement (legit_wallet_help: suite expects review, the model chose no-action to avoid burdening a help-seeker) is preserved rather than tuned away — it is a live policy question, and pretending the tools agree would hide it.
